scope_note: QA Cayley-Hamilton Fibonacci-Lucas cert [299]. Primary sources: Hardy, G.H. and Wright, E.M. (2008) An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers, Oxford, ISBN 978-0-19-921986-5, Ch.X (Fibonacci recurrence, matrix representation); Lucas, E. (1878) Theorie des fonctions numeriques simplement periodiques, American Journal of Mathematics 1(2):184-196, DOI 10.2307/2369308 (Lucas numbers); Wall, D.D. (1960) Fibonacci Primitive Roots, Amer. Math. Monthly 67(6):525-532 DOI 10.1080/00029890.1960.11989541 (Pisano period). CLAIM (narrow, falsifiable): Let M=[[0,1],[1,1]] be the QA T-operator. (C1) Cayley-Hamilton identity: M^2=M+I exactly as integer matrices (characteristic polynomial x^2-x-1, verified by direct computation). (C2) Fibonacci matrix entries: M^k=[[F(k-1),F(k)],[F(k),F(k+1)]] for all k>=0, where F is the Fibonacci sequence with F(0)=0, F(1)=1; verified k=0..40 by induction and direct matrix power. (C3) Lucas trace formula: Tr(M^k)=F(k-1)+F(k+1)=L(k) for all k>=1, where L is the Lucas sequence L(1)=1,L(2)=3,L(n)=L(n-1)+L(n-2); verified k=1..40. (C4) Alternating determinant: det(M^k)=(-1)^k for all k>=0; follows from det(M)=-1 and det(M^k)=det(M)^k. (C5) Cayley-Hamilton corollary of [298]: Tr(M^12)=L(12)=322; 322 mod 9=7=-2 mod 9=Tr(-I mod 9), consistent with cert [298] (M^12=-I mod 9). Also: M^24=I mod 9 gives Tr(M^24)=L(24) mod 9 = 2 = Tr(I). Mechanism: Theorem NT maintained; all computations on integer matrices. Builds on cert [291] (M order 24 mod 9), cert [296] (T=M), cert [298] (M^12=-I mod 9). Lineage: derived 2026-06-02.
